(i).question:
Define experience rating and describe why it is used for determining the premium for
             most employer‐sponsored group health insurance contracts.                            [3]

A group medical expenses scheme is being reviewed prior to renewal. The renewal is due on 1st
July 2018 and the following data are available as at 31st March 2018.

Large claims

Renewal            Number of    Reported         Number          Amount (£)
Year             employees    claims (£)
at renewal
date

## 2014                  875       87,100            0                         0

## 2015                  900      130,130            1                  27,000

## 2016                  950      165,500            1                  39,000

## 2017                1,000      119,500            0                         0

The reported claims amounts are based on claims that have been reported to date in the renewal
year and so include estimates of outstanding reported claims.

The large claims have been included in the reported claims amounts. It should be assumed that
inflation is constant at 10% pa throughout and that there are expected to be 1,000 employees in
the scheme at the renewal date in 2018. The insurer is using experience rating, with the past
experience component being based on the period of data given.

Definition and rationale for use of expe: Experience rating is a system whereby the premium for a group contract depends wholly or
partially on the past claims experience of that risk.                                                   [½]
A credibility factor Z , where 0  Z  1 , defines the proportion of the risk premium that relates to
the past experience of the group.                                                                  [½]
This factor will be larger for schemes with more stable / predictable claims experience, eg those
with more employees or lower staff turnover rates.                                              [½]
A book rate assessment of a company’s employee health and care contract may be inappropriate,
due, for example, to the:                                                                 [¼]
         peculiarities of a specific workplace                                                         [¼]
         working processes of the company                                                              [¼]
         employer’s attitude to safety.                                                                [¼]
The group’s past claims history, suitably adjusted to make it relevant to the coming policy term, is
therefore possibly the best predictor of expected future claims.                                [½]
This will hopefully make the premium more competitive …                                                 [½]
… and reduce the need for a large number of rating factors, so that it is more straightforward to
price.                                                                                           [½]
However, experience rating would be unsuitable where the group’s past claims experience is
insufficient or unreliable for use in setting the premium – for example for a new or small group.
[Maximum 3]

(ii).question:
Describe, with the help of numerical examples, the steps in the calculation of the renewal
premium (both per employee and for the scheme in total).

Renewal premium calculation steps: Note that reasonable credit would be awarded for points made with detailed numerical examples
even if the figures are not precisely the same as given in this specimen solution.
The first step is to adjust the data in respect of renewal year 2017, which are only in respect of
claims reported during a 9‐month period, to give a full year’s data, eg:
0.75: This assumes that claims are reported evenly throughout the year …                                      [½]
… and ignores IBNR claims (which may be reasonable because the reporting delay will be small for
Given that PMI is a short‐tail class of business, it is also reasonable to assume that years prior to
2017 are complete, and hence no further adjustment to these data are needed.                        [½]
Next, the data should be adjusted for large claims. These should be stripped out of the base data,
One way of doing this would be to use a grossing‐up factor based on the ratio of large claims to all
reported claims for all similar group policies.                                                 [½]
A grossing‐up factor based upon this group’s data alone (as this is all that is available) can be
calculated from the total claims cost divided by the total cost less the large claims, ie:

be inflated, assuming that on average claims occur mid year.
Next the data should be examined for tre: Renewal            Employees         Cost in        Average        % rise

continue then it will need to be projected forward to cover the next period of exposure.      [½]
The trend is from the midpoint of the claim analysis to the midpoint of the period that the rates
will be in force for, ie 2.5 years.                                                              [½]
The remaining steps in calculating the r:          Consider the book rate premium for this group (E), appropriate to the prospective
This will depend on factors such as the age and (possibly) gender profile of the
membership and group risk factors such as location and industry.                              [½]
         Determine the credibility factor appropriate for this group (Z).                              [½]

(iii).question:
Describe how you would determine the additional parameters you will need for the
calculations you have set out in part (ii)(a) (ie in addition to those you may have illustrated
or described already).

Factors to consider include:          significant changes of personnel, changes of location, changes of work practices or
         office practice for other similar risks                                                           [¼]
Expense loadings:          If claims handling expenses are not included in the claim costs, then a loading must be
made in the premium for them.                                                           [¼]
         Renewal costs could be derived from a recent expense analysis.                                    [¼]
         Contribution to fixed expenses will take account of any commission payments and
         Expected expense inflation over the period should be allowed for.                           [¼]

(iv).question:
State any specific conditions that the insurer might apply to a group medical expenses
scheme.

The insurer might specify that there are: The premium will be subject to adjustment at the end of the year based on the actual number of
Pre‐existing conditions might be excluded, depending on the underwriting terms offered.                     [½]
Joining of the scheme may only be permitted after a specified period in work, eg one year.                  [½]
Other standard health insurance exclusions would normally apply, ...                                        [½]
... eg dangerous sports, illegal activities, self‐inflicted injury, drug abuse, normal pregnancy,
cosmetic surgery, AIDS related illnesses.                       [¼ each for any two sensible suggestions]

18.2.label: Question 18.2
18.2.scenario:
An existing group health and care insurance scheme, which provides IP benefits, operates under

the following conditions:
         membership is compulsory for all employees who are actively at work on the first
anniversary of starting work with their employer
         membership ceases immediately if the employee leaves employment for any reason
         a level of free cover is granted to all new members
         premiums are experience rated with a credibility factor of 0.2.

Discuss the expected effects of the following on the claims experience under the scheme. You
can assume that each occurs without any of the others occurring at the same time.

(a).question: Raising the free cover to 150% of the current level.

Raising the free cover level: This will increase the cost of claims from members who present higher than standard risks.             [½]
However, because membership is compulsory and new employees (one year before membership)
are likely to be healthy, the financial impact of this is likely to be limited and, with adequate data,

(b).question:
An increase in the number of voluntary withdrawals from the scheme, through members
resigning from their employment.

Increase in voluntary withdrawals: It is likely that the healthiest lives will leave, ...                                                 [½]
... which will increase the expected claims experience amongst the remaining members.                  [½]

(c).question: Changing from compulsory to voluntary membership.

Changing from compulsory to voluntary me: This increases the chance of anti‐selection – with a greater proportion of the higher‐risk
individuals electing to join the scheme compared to those of lower risk.                               [½]
The existence of the free cover level makes this prospect more serious, as cases below the free
cover level are accepted on standard terms regardless of their state of health.                 [½]
The result could be a worsening of the claims experience amongst those who do choose to take
… although it is mitigated by the fact that decisions on joining still have to made only a year after
starting work, and so relatively few employees will be able to recognise a marked increase in their
… and it will be further reduced if the membership take‐up rate turns out to be high.                  [½]

(d).question: An increase in unemployment in the industry in which the employer operates.

Increase in unemployment: If the employer is laying off some of the workforce, it would be unlikely for the healthiest lives to
be those who were made redundant. So there is unlikely to be any adverse selection effect
However, the employment conditions may lead to higher claim costs amongst those still in
… for example, the employer may attempt to reduce the cost of unproductive labour by
encouraging existing claimants to claim benefits for longer, ...                                       [½]
... or it may actively use it as a redundancy vehicle.                                                 [½]

Increasing the credibility factor: The premiums will become more responsive to the company’s ongoing experience.                          [½]
Elements of moral hazard (such as the examples given in (d) above) are likely to be more
controlled by the employer, wherever possible, thus improving the claims experience.         [½]

18.3.label: Question 18.3
18.3.scenario:
A health and care insurance company is currently in the process of re‐pricing its group IP product.
   The company uses experience rating for policies that have more than 1,000 person‐years of past
experience data.

(i).question:
Describe the investigations the company will carry out in order to determine assumptions
for use in the re‐pricing of these contracts, including an explanation of how the
assumptions will be determined. (A detailed description of an expense investigation is
not required.)

Investigations required and setting assu: The company will need to analyse the experience of its portfolio of policies as a whole, so that it
can work out a table of book rates for the product.                                               [½]
It will also need to analyse separately the relevant past experience for each policy (scheme) that
satisfies the conditions for experience rating.                                                  [½]
It will need to analyse: It will also require assumptions for future investment returns and inflation.                            [½]
Book rates will differ on account of the:          deferred period and other contractual differences.                                             [½]
To estimate the book rates, the data for all schemes will be combined and analysed according to
An appropriate investigation period will be chosen: as long as possible in order to maximise the
... but not so long as to introduce significant heterogeneity into the experience.                       [½]
Claim inception rates will be analysed by comparing actual numbers of inceptions with those
expected according to the current book rate assumptions.                                                 [½]
Alternatively a standard table could be used for this (if available).                                    [½]
The data will be further subdivided into:          duration in employment (or since joining the scheme)                                         [¼]
Subdivision by category of employer (eg manual, non‐manual), if appropriate, might also be
A similar analysis would be done for claim termination rates. An additional key factor for
subdivision will be duration of sickness or claim payments.                                           [½]
Historical experience rates would be adjusted, if necessary.                                          [½]
This would be in order to take account o:          underwriting standards for cases in excess of the free cover limits                         [½]
         trends in experience (reinsurers’ and industry data may also be used for this)              [½]
         changes in distribution channel or target market                                            [½]
         past, current and future expected economic conditions (which could influence the
employees’ propensity to claim either individually or as a result of their employer’s
Any expected reduction in termination rates would be included in the assumptions used for
The experience rates, for each scheme entitled to experience rating, would be determined by a
similar analysis, using the data for that company only.                                       [½]
For a particular experience‐rated scheme, the individual risk premium rates would then be
obtained by combining the book rates and the rates based on its own past experience, using the
appropriate credibility factors for the scheme.                                              [½]
Renewal rates: Recent historical rates of renewal would be analysed, broken down by occupation, territory, sales
channel, and duration since the company acquired the scheme.                                   [½]
The ratios of numbers of policies (schemes) renewing over the numbers coming up for renewal
Future renewal rates would then be projected.                                                         [½]
The projection would take into account:          past, present and future economic conditions                                                   [½]
         the relative competitiveness of the proposed rates being charged, and whether this is
The recent experience will be analysed i:          number of members in the scheme                                                                [½]
         as a percentage of premium                                                                     [½]
         as a percentage of benefit level                                                               [½]
         per policy (scheme).                                                                           [½]
Dividing by the appropriate measures gives the expense loadings for the contract.                        [½]
This outline of the expense investigation is sufficient given the comment in the question, but you
do still need to describe how the results of the investigation will be used:
The loadings will then need to be increased for inflation to the middle of the period for which
they are expected to be appropriate.                                                            [½]
The past expenses (and loadings) will need to be split according to whether they were direct or
overhead.                                                                                       [½]
In order to estimate its expense inflation rate, the company would compare its historical expense
increases against past changes in prices and earnings indices.                                 [½]
Expectations of relevant index‐inflation rates will help to determine the rate to use.                   [½]
An allowance will be made for spreading the initial expenses over the expected number of
renewals of the contract – using the appropriate renewal rate assumptions.                               [½]
Commission would be allowed at the rate(s) that the company intends to pay.                              [½]
The additional cost of initial commission would be spread over the expected renewals identically
to the other initial expenses.                                                                 [½]
The proper per‐policy (scheme) expense assumptions would be obtained by considering total
expected future new business volumes, including both new business and renewals, to work out
each policy’s expected contribution.                                                       [½]
Investment return / risk discount rate: An investment return assumption will be needed for discounting the claim annuity payments. [½]
This will be based on the real yields from secure index‐linked investments (or fixed interest if the
benefits are not inflating) of matching durations.                                                 [½]
Margins: Finally, margins will need to be added, but because the business is annually renewable the
company is able to review its premiums every year, ...                                               [½]
... and so the level of margins may not need to be large, provided the company does review its
The margins are taken, either explicitly on the individual assumptions themselves, or incorporated

(ii).question:
Outline the additional factors that the company should consider before using the new
premium rates.

Additional factors: This may be a price‐competitive market.                                                              [½]
Premium rates of competing companies may constrain the rates the company charges or affect
options or guarantees that it needs to give.                                               [½]
They will also affect the assumptions of new business volumes and renewal rates.                     [½]
The morbidity assumptions will be affected by the extent of any guarantees given on premium
Availability of reinsurance on favourable terms may reduce any margins needed in the premiums.
High reinsurance costs may lead to higher premiums.                                                  [½]
If the insurer issues associated group pension schemes to the same clients it could reduce the
expense loadings in its group IP rates.                                                        [½]

(i).question: State the reasons why group PMI policies may be experience rated.

Risk with a cross\u2010subsidy approach: The risk is that the mix of business will differ adversely from expected. For example, the insurer
may find it easier to sell the (possibly loss‐making) cheaper product, so sell more of it, while
volumes of the more expensive product may be inadequate to cover the shortfall.

(ii).question:
Explain the concept of experience rating as it applies to group PMI policies, and no‐claims
discount systems as they apply to individual PMI policies. You should also mention the
similarities and differences between them.

Solution: These assumptions should be the same as the reserving assumptions that the insurer expects to
use in the future (as determined by regulation).
So, the reserving assumptions used in the pricing model should be prudent if that is the type of
basis that the insurer will realistically expect to use for setting up its reserves in the future.
However, the reserving assumptions used in the pricing model should be best estimates if
regulations specify best estimate reserves.
The pricing model should also allow for any solvency capital requirements (which could be
substantial, particularly if best estimate assumptions are used for reserves).
Some companies, which are adequately capitalised, decide not to charge some policies with
the cost of their solvency capital requirement. This is done to make the pricing of this line
of business as competitive as possible.
An insurance company that ignores the cost of capital in this way has to be careful that it doesn’t
assume that a particular product is more profitable than it is. This is particularly important if the
insurance company is trying to compare the merits of alternative product designs, if the reserving
(and/or solvency capital) requirements of these products differ substantially.
Solvency capital requirements may be reduced by suitable reinsurance arrangements.
Pure reinsurers may have lower solvency capital requirements than direct writers, and as
such the higher the proportion reinsured, the lower the total solvency capital required.
So as well as reducing the insurer’s capital requirement, the total capital required by the industry
to support a particular policy is reduced. It would be hoped that some of the cost savings from
this could be fed back to the consumer by charging lower premiums. It would therefore seem
appropriate to allow for any (financial) reinsurance in the insurer’s pricing models (see Chapter 26
on reinsurance for more details).
The reinsurer will have initially agreed with the insurer what it considers to be an appropriate
premium scale for the business. The reinsurer’s commission (ie paid by the reinsurer to the
insurer) is in fair compensation for the underwriting, marketing and selling costs that the insurer
incurs on the reinsurer’s behalf, and this would also be agreed initially.
Should the insurer now wish to reduce (say) its premium rates (eg for competitive reasons), it
might (depending on the type of reinsurance purchased) affect the premiums received by the
reinsurer in proportion to the percentage reinsured. In order to leave the reinsurer in the same
financial position as before the change, the reinsurer would probably demand a reduction in the
commission payable to compensate.
Don’t worry if you didn’t answer this question fully on your first attempt. You will find out more
about reinsurance later in the course – so it may be worth you having another go at this question
later, once you have read the relevant chapter.
This is because any significant deterioration in future experience can be covered by future rate
rises. Non‐reviewable rates do not permit this luxury and therefore need much larger margins to
cover possible future adverse experience (that may occur in many years’ time).
Generally, the size of the margins will depend on how frequently reviews are carried out.
Where an increase in premium is called for, actuaries must be careful not to encourage
selective lapsing. The basis of their calculations will be invalid if, as a result of the change,
a significant proportion of the more healthy lives decline to proceed and the risk under the
balance of the portfolio escalates as a result. This is likely if lives can reapply to a
competitor and thus reinstate their coverage more cheaply.
The extent to which the effectiveness of this rate review may be compromised by selective
lapsing must therefore be taken into account when deciding by how much rates should be
increased. Increasing the rates by too much can backfire on the insurer, by leaving it holding a
smaller portfolio with a much higher per‐policy expense cost than before, negating some or all of
the hoped‐for beneficial effect of the rate rise.
In some territories, the regulator has given guidance as to what aspects of the premium rate
basis may be considered in order to justify a change in reviewable premium rates.
Covering this risk for a long\u2010term: The risk may be partly covered by having reviewable premiums on the loss‐leading product or by
having adequate free assets to cover the potential shortfall.
Over time, and in line with emerging experience, it is to be expected that each product is
brought back in line to produce its own contribution to designated profits. At this stage
profits will be largely immune from business mix fluctuations.
3.4 Overall return on capital: The key measure required is that when such adjustments have been made, the required
return on capital at each profit centre is met. If this does not happen, then there are serious
grounds for withdrawing from that particular class of business. An excess return on capital
may indicate that the pricing basis in one or more subsets can be relaxed to promote
A profit centre can be any collection of business items that has a separate target for contribution
to insurer profit or revenue. At one extreme it could be very broadly defined (eg all of the
insurer’s broker‐produced business), or at the other extreme very narrowly defined (eg the book
of female stand‐alone CI policies with buy‐back options sold by a tied aged in Southampton).
4 Reserving and solvency capital requi: Within many territories, the writing of a policy creates an additional need for regulatory
reserves and solvency capital requirements. Such a capital requirement can be expressed
as a percentage of premium, sum at risk or basic policy reserves, or may be a risk-based
capital calculation. It is thus appropriate to charge each new policy for the cost of this
capital.
Remember that all of the supervisory reserve for a policy, as well as the additional amount
required for the minimum solvency capital requirement, ties up capital in the business.
The charge is the investment cost arising from holding the underlying assets as ‘locked in’
statutory capital rather than investing them directly in business acquisition. Simplistically,
if a policy has an internal rate of return of 10% per annum and statutory capital is invested
in assets that yield 4% per annum, the charge to the policy will effectively be a negative
cashflow of 6% per annum of the amount of these solvency capital requirements for as long
as they are required. (In practice, there are other ‘frictional cost’ considerations such as
taxation.)
The key point here is that the supervisory reserves, including all additional solvency capital
requirements, should be fully incorporated into the pricing model. For the cost of capital to be
accurately modelled, this means that:
          the reserves included in the pricing model should be calculated using the assumptions
that the insurer would be realistically expected to make, under the assumed future
conditions implied by the pricing model
          the rate of interest at which the net profits are discounted (the risk discount rate) fully
allows for the returns required by shareholders on the capital they have invested.
Question: Will the assumptions used for calculating the reserves in the pricing model be on a best estimate
or a prudent basis?
Why would the reinsurer expect the right to vary reinsurance commission in these circumstances?
Why do reviewable rates include lower margins than non‐reviewable ones?
5.1 Overall cost of reinsurance: The role of reinsurance can be very important in the day-to-day management of product
development and profitability. It can provide a number of roles in this area:
          technical assistance in data provision and pricing basis, which is particularly
important if this is a new product area
\uf0b7 providing financing to s:           tax arbitrage where the reinsurer is taxed on a different basis from the insurer
          solvency capital arbitrage where the reinsurer is required to hold less capital per
If any of the above services are deemed to have a cost, ie situations where the direct
monetary gain is exceeded by the reduction in premium, actuaries should include this as an
Unless some arbitrage is possible (which it often is) then the insurer will expect to make a loss
from the reinsurance it takes out, as the value of the reinsurance premiums paid will be greater
than the expected present value of the reinsurance benefits payable (remember that the
reinsurer has to cover its own expenses and profit). This loss, which is effectively a reduction in
the expected profits that would be earned from the policy without reinsurance, would have to be
Assumptions would need to be made about the future mix of business (eg by policy size) in order
to assess the average amount of reinsurance per policy. Alternatively, and especially if the
amount of reinsurance increases with policy size, it may be possible to have differential premiums
or charging rates depending on the size of the benefits provided under the policy.
Note that further discussion of the role of reinsurance can be found in Chapter 26.
Some kind of arbitrage is generally nece: Where the financial benefits of reinsurance exceed the expense increase or premium loss,
eg by tax arbitrage, the actuary is likely to want to price the product on a net of reinsurance
basis to ensure that premiums are as competitive as possible.
This is really just the same as described in Section 5.1 above, except with the opposite financial
5.3 Reinsurer approval: The relationship between insurer and reinsurer may be such that a large portion of the risk
is passed to the reinsurer by way of quota share. This may well arise when the insurer has
no expertise in the new product area and is depending substantially on reinsurer expertise
Quota share reinsurance is where a fixed proportion of all premiums and claims under a product
line are transferred to the reinsurer.
Under these circumstances, it is normal for the reinsurer to have a major say in the
production of premium tables, or at least to have the right to vary any reinsurance
commission payable, following a revision of tariffs.
6.1 Pricing freedom: The role of the regulator varies by territory. Where the insurer has a role in the provision of
welfare payments, it is likely that the pricing basis for such insurances will be closely
monitored by the supervisors – indeed the basis may be totally prescribed.
Actuaries may have to submit their basis and calculations to the supervisory authority
before the policies can be marketed.
In other instances, only certain aspects of pricing may be restricted, eg a single premium
cost to all new subscribers (community rating) or the inability to differentiate between risk
Here, a ‘single premium’ cost means a single rate of premium that would be applied to all new
policyholders. It does not mean the condition applies only to single premiums (ie as opposed to
Actuaries will include such conditions in their premium calculations, as well as using their
expertise to explore ways in which any consumer detriment resulting from the restrictions
6.2 Cost impact: Alternatively, the regulator might restrict the way in which the insurer can operate.
An example would be the restriction imposed in many territories on the kind of information
insurers can use for underwriting. Insurers may be prevented from taking into account the results
of any genetic tests that insurance applicants may have had.
Any regulatory restriction on commercial operations may impose a cost that needs to be
reflected in the premium assumptions. For example:
          a requirement to invest in ‘local’ assets may impact the investment return that can
          the need for the insurer to collect premium taxes will also necessitate a further
expense loading in the premium basis.
Such additions to costs will affect all insurers in a market equally and thus should not affect
\uf0b7 monitoring the assumption:          monitoring the assumptions used for pricing reviewable premium business
         monitoring whether the reserves set up to cover losses from loss‐leaders are adequate.
7.1 Regular monitoring of new business: The pricing of new business will require the most up-to-date information. Actuaries will
keep all items in their assumption sets under review. When any one of these is no longer
valid, the effect of this must be tested against the existing premium tariff to see whether the
charging rate for new business needs to be changed.
This is a vital part of the actuarial control cycle, and is the means by which the actuary ensures the
pricing models are up to date and valid. The Core Reading is suggesting that not every change in
the assumptions will necessarily lead to a change in the prices the insurer actually charges.
Remember that changing prices incurs the insurer in much expense, and also may have (possibly
undesirable) effects on marketability. These effects have to be weighed up against the changes in
profitability and risk caused by the altered conditions when deciding whether and/or how the
A related issue is how deviations in past experience from expected lead to changes in the
company’s estimates of its future experience. See Chapters 12‐16 (covering data and
assumptions) and also Chapter 29 (on monitoring) for more discussion about this.
Information leading to changes in the assumptions may come from internal ‘experience’
7.2 Regular monitoring of reviewable p: The pricing actuary will also be involved in the estimation of premiums where these are
periodically reviewed as part of the contract terms. For long-term healthcare insurance
products, the rates will require revision if the experience under the portfolio has differed
markedly from that assumed since the previous review.
The reason why the insurer needs to monitor its reviewable premium rates closely is due to the
much smaller margins that are typically included in such rates.
Example: In the UK, there is significant guidance given to insurers regarding reviewability of long‐term
Among the conditions that insurers must:           the basis for reviewing premiums should be clearly set out in policy literature
          premiums can only be increased if the insurer changes one or more of the relevant
assumptions in this premium basis for ‘valid reason’
          where premiums are changed as a result of claims experience changes, it must be clear
that this does not relate to the experience of the individual policyholder, but to general
7.3 Premium adequacy tested for reserv: Where a policy is sold at less than the full premium rate for the expected claims and
expense outgo, for portfolio development or cross-subsidy reasons, reserves will need to
be established for the extent to which the pricing basis is inadequate.
Unless there are regulations that require minimum premiums and charges to be levied for a
particular policy, the insurer would be free to undercharge on its insurance contracts, eg for
marketing reasons. At the same time, it would still have to reserve adequately for the risks taken
on allowing for the future premium that is actually being paid.
The size of the reserves will depend on:          the speed at which the premium can be reviewed (and its size upgraded to the full
         the likelihood that policyholders will renew their contracts as the premium level is
So, if the premium is inadequate, the insurer will have to find capital from other sources in order
to set up the necessary reserves. The capital may come from shareholders or from retained
However, where inadequate rates apply on long-term insurances on guaranteed terms, there
are serious considerations for the actuaries responsible; their course of action will depend
on the codes of professional guidance in the territory where they operate.
Profit criteria: Profit criteria are single figures that try to summarise the profit signature. Three commonly
\uf0b7 discounted payback perio: The net present value is calculated by discounting the profit signature at the risk discount
rate. In theory, the net present value is the best profit criterion to use, however, it is
dependent on the risk discount rate being appropriate for the inherent risk.
\uf0b7 total discounted premium: The internal rate of return is defined as the rate of return at which the discounted value of
the cashflow is zero. It has some disadvantages in comparison with the net present value as
\uf0b7 it might not exist / it:           it cannot be related to other indicators such as sales cost or premium income.
The discounted payback period is defined as the policy duration at which the profits that
have emerged so far have a present value of zero. It is a useful reference for companies
eager to recoup their initial capital investment in as short a time as possible.
Marketability: The premiums (or charges) produced will be considered for marketability. This might lead to
Competitiveness: Rating factors need to be consistent with competition to avoid/reduce anti‐selection risk.
Volume is important to cover fixed expenses. Premiums may need to be reduced in order to
Chapter 19 Summary continued: Inadequate premiums may be charged – eg for a ‘loss‐leading’ product – however,
loss‐leader products must be compensated by larger margins in other lines of business. Over
time, each product is expected to be brought back in line to produce a more fair contribution
A product line should aim to meet return on capital requirements, taking all cross‐subsidies
Reserving and solvency capital requireme: Premium rates should explicitly include the cost of holding the supervisory reserves and
required solvency capital in the profit calculations. Solvency capital requirements may be
reduced by the use of suitable reinsurance arrangements.
Reinsurance impact: Reinsurance can alter expected profitability. Any material impact needs to be incorporated
Insurers often receive pricing advice from their reinsurers, who then earn a right to influence
the prices the insurer can charge. This is particularly true when the insurer and reinsurer
have proportionate shares of premiums received and claims paid.
Regulatory constraints: Pricing bases, premium rates, or rating factors can be put under direct control of the
The regulator can also affect pricing bases indirectly by restricting insurer operation
(eg through constraints on investment strategy or underwriting). These will affect the
pricing assumptions and hence the price that will be charged.
Reviews: Premium bases must be kept up to date through frequent experience monitoring.
New business premium rates should be tested against any changes to the current basis /
assumptions. Premium rate changes would be considered in the light of the financial impact
of a basis change, but bearing in mind other factors such as expense and marketability.
Many health and care insurance products avoid the potentially large cost of guarantees by
having reviewable charging rates. The premium may be subject to review on a regular basis
and adjusted when overall experience is judged to have changed significantly.
Reviewability increases the risk of selective lapsing, which may reduce the beneficial effects
